did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning approach, specifically a MobileNetV2-based convolutional neural network (CNN), to classify skin lesions (benign vs. malignant) from the HAM10000 dataset. The method incorporates an image shifting technique on a square lattice to enhance the classification performance.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing an enhanced transfer learning model using image shifting on a square lattice for skin lesion classification. It details a specific methodology (MobileNetV2-based CNN), describes experiments conducted on the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets, and reports quantitative results (accuracy, TPR, F1 scores) comparing the proposed method against raw models. The structure includes Introduction, Methodology, Experimental Results, and Conclusions, which is characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Enhanced transfer learning model by image shifting on a square lattice for skin lesion malignancy assessment
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying skin lesions as benign or malignant to support physicians in diagnosis. The authors propose an enhanced transfer learning model based on MobileNetV2 that incorporates an image shifting technique on a square lattice to improve classification performance. The method was evaluated using the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,050 dermoscopic images of benign and malignant moles. Experiments utilized 10-fold cross-validation with metrics including True Positive Rate, True Negative Rate, Positive Predictive Value, and F1-score. The results demonstrate that the proposed shifting technique enhances classification accuracy and reduces false negatives compared to raw transfer learning models without requiring re-training.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes used in the classification task. In the HAM10000 dataset, this class contains more than 7,000 images. In the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, it is one of six classes but noted to have a small number of elements.

The second binary class in the study's primary classification task, grouping all malignant or abnormal lesions. In HAM10000, this group contains around 3,000 images of abnormal moles. In PAD-UFES-20, it comprises the other five classes.
Listed as one of the six classes present in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, but not individually studied as a separate target class in the binary classification experiments.
Listed as one of the six classes present in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset and mentioned in the introduction as a prevalent skin cancer, but not individually studied as a separate target class.
Listed as 'seborrheic keratosis' in PAD-UFES-20 and 'benign keratosis' in HAM10000. It is part of the 'non-nevus' group in the study's binary classification task.
Listed as one of the six classes in PAD-UFES-20 and mentioned in the introduction as a prevalent skin cancer, but not individually studied as a separate target class.
Listed as one of the six classes present in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, grouped into the 'non-nevus' class for the study's experiments.
Listed as one of the six classes in PAD-UFES-20 and one of the six malignant classes in HAM10000. It is grouped into the 'non-nevus' (mel) class for the study's binary classification task.
Listed as one of the six classes in the HAM10000 dataset, grouped into the 'non-nevus' class for the study's experiments.
Listed as 'vascular skin' in the HAM10000 dataset description, grouped into the 'non-nevus' class for the study's experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign (nevi) or non-benign (malignant/abnormal) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is to 'discriminate benign and non-benign skin diseases' and 'classify skin disease images on the two-classes: nevi or others'. The model outputs class scores for these discrete categories using a MobileNetV2-based CNN. Evaluation metrics include True Positive Rate, True Negative Rate, Positive Predictive Value, and F1-score, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, collected from institutions in Austria and Australia. It was employed for the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 challenge and serves as a benchmark for classification models.

use.training: Used for the main performance evaluation of the proposed shifting model. Data was split using 10-fold cross-validation with 70% for training.
use.validation: Used for validation during 10-fold cross-validation with 10% of samples.
use.testing: Used for testing during 10-fold cross-validation with 20% of samples.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A dataset composed of clinical smartphone images and patient data collected by the University of Espirito Santo (Brazil). It contains images of six different classes and was used to study the accuracy of the proposal with completely unseen data.
classes_used: mel (non-nevus, comprising actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma, and Bowen’s disease)

use.testing: Used for external comparison/testing with completely unseen data to evaluate the model's generalization.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide a precise comparison of the models and evaluate the performance of the proposed shifting model against the raw MobileNetV2 model.
To present the classification results (True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, False Negatives) for each test set split in the cross-validation.
To summarize the overall performance of the models by computing the average of metrics (TPR, TNR, PPV, NPV, Accuracy, F1-score) across all 10 splits.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using average metrics (TPR, TNR, PPV, NPV, Accuracy, F1-score) derived from 10-fold cross-validation and confusion matrices.

HAM10000.notes: The shifting model showed an enhancement of 1.5% in average TPR and an increase of around 1% in Accuracy and F1-score compared to the raw model. The raw model had high false positives for the nevi class. The shifting model significantly reduced false negatives for the mel class.

PAD-UFES-20.experiment: External testing on completely unseen data
PAD-UFES-20.notes: The shifting method improved performance by around 2-2.5% in accuracy and F-measure on unseen data. It reduced the number of false negatives and correctly detected almost all nevi. The raw model yielded incorrect nevi predictions for challenging images (e.g., PAT 1070 304 845 and PAT 1184 678 86) with low mean scores (62.26% and 71.54% respectively), whereas the shifting procedure allowed better prediction.

components: Shortcut connections (similar to ResNet) to eliminate non-linearity and maintain representation

shifting_technique.method: Image shifting on a square lattice
shifting_technique.description: Input image X is circularly shifted by displacement vectors s on a square lattice with stride R. Combined class scores are computed as the average of network outputs for N shifted versions of the input.
shifting_technique.lattice_size: Constrained to avoid duplicity, centered in the middle of the image

classification_or_output_head.mechanism: Average of outputs F(X ⊕ si) for N shifted inputs

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 224 x 224 pixels to meet MobileNetV2 input requirements
Data augmentation: random rotations between -90° and 90° (probability 0.75)
Image shifting on a square lattice (circular shifts with stride R=4 and max displacement M=40 pixels) to generate multiple variants of the input image for combined class score estimation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied between -90° and 90° with a probability of 0.75.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Image shifting on a square lattice
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Circular shifts of the input image using displacement vectors on a square lattice with stride R=4 pixels and maximum displacement M=40 pixels to generate multiple variants for combined class score estimation.

conclusions: The proposed MobileNetV2-based model enhanced with an image shifting technique on a square lattice provides better performance than raw transfer learning models for skin lesion classification.
The shifting technique improves classification accuracy and reduces false negatives compared to the raw MobileNetV2 model, which is critical for medical diagnosis to avoid missing malignant lesions.

On the HAM10000 dataset, the shifting model achieved an average True Positive Rate (TPR) of 81.22% and an accuracy of 87.49%, representing an enhancement of 1.5% in TPR and approximately 1% in accuracy and F1-score compared to the raw model.
The shifting method allows for improved performance on completely unseen data, as demonstrated by the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, where it improved accuracy by around 2-2.5% and reduced false negatives.
The proposed technique can be applied to state-of-the-art deep models to improve their results without requiring re-training of the network.
The raw MobileNetV2 model was found to be highly dependent on the training set images, showing disparities in classification performance across different cross-validation splits.

The shifting model effectively handles challenging cases where the raw model fails, such as images with low mean scores or ambiguous features, by leveraging multiple shifted versions of the input to compute combined class scores.
important_remarks: The HAM10000 dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with approximately 7,000 benign (nevi) images versus 3,000 malignant (non-nevi) images, which may bias the model towards the majority class.

The raw MobileNetV2 model showed high dependency on the specific training split, with performance disparities across 10-fold cross-validation splits (e.g., false negative rates varying significantly), indicating instability without the proposed shifting technique.

External validation on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset revealed poor generalization, with the shifting model achieving only 61.10% accuracy and 57.59% TPR, suggesting limited clinical applicability to unseen data from different acquisition sources (smartphone vs. dermoscopy).

The PAD-UFES-20 dataset used for external testing has a very small number of nevi samples, which may lead to imprecise positive predictions and unreliable evaluation of the model's ability to detect benign lesions in that specific context.
The study did not perform formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to confirm the significance of the performance improvements, relying instead on average metrics from cross-validation.

The proposed shifting technique is a post-processing inference-time modification that does not require re-training, but its effectiveness is constrained by the input image size (224x224) and the assumption that lesions are centrally located with a margin of 40 pixels.

The binary classification task groups diverse malignant pathologies (melanoma, BCC, SCC, etc.) into a single 'non-nevi' class, preventing the model from distinguishing between different types of skin cancer, which limits its specific diagnostic utility.
